title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 12

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which mechanized textile production and global cotton demand transformed labor systems and regional economies from c. 1750 to 1900.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Petition from handloom weavers to a local revenue official, Bengal, 1817.
Source Type: Artisan petition.
“Our fathers wove cloth for merchants who knew our villages and paid advances before the season. Now imported machine-made cloth sells cheaply in the bazaar, and Company agents demand payments while thread prices rise. Some families abandon the loom for field labor; others borrow money and fall into debt. The skill of many years has become a burden.”
Sourcing cues: Artisan perspective; emphasizes deindustrialization, debt, and competition from machine-made textiles.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Speech by a textile mill owner to a manufacturers’ association, Manchester, 1834.
Source Type: Business speech.
“The future of industry depends upon uninterrupted cotton supply, disciplined factory labor, and access to markets abroad. Machines allow one town to clothe millions, but only if raw cotton arrives cheaply and if foreign governments do not close their ports to our goods.”
Sourcing cues: Industrial capitalist perspective; emphasizes machinery, raw materials, labor discipline, and export markets.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Letter from an Egyptian village headman to a provincial official during the cotton boom, 1863.
Source Type: Local administrative letter.
“Merchants offer high prices for cotton, and officials say the crop will enrich the country. Yet cotton takes land and water that once grew grain for village use. Laborers are called to clean canals, and taxes are assessed before the harvest is sold. We do not oppose profit, but hunger follows when every field is ordered toward the market.”
Sourcing cues: Local rural official; shows tension between export agriculture, taxation, irrigation labor, and subsistence needs.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Export summary compiled by a commercial newspaper, 1860–1885.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Cotton-Related Economic Patterns, 1860–1885
Region Reported Pattern
United States Civil War disrupts cotton exports in the 1860s
Egypt Cotton exports increase sharply during the American Civil War
India Raw cotton exports rise while handloom producers face pressure
Brazil Cotton and textile production expand unevenly by region
Russia / Central Asia Officials encourage cotton cultivation for imperial textile needs
Note on the table: Merchants reported that disruptions in one cotton-producing region encouraged expansion or investment in other regions.
Sourcing cues: Commercial newspaper perspective; highlights market shifts but compresses regional social differences.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Report from a Russian official in Turkestan to the Ministry of Finance, 1889.
Source Type: Government report.
“Cotton cultivation in the irrigated districts should be expanded to reduce dependence on foreign supply. Rail connections and credit will encourage growers to plant more cotton for Russian mills. Care must be taken that grain does not disappear from local markets, but the empire cannot remain at the mercy of distant producers.”
Sourcing cues: Imperial official perspective; links cotton cultivation to state economic strategy and imperial integration.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a photograph of a cotton mill in Bombay, 1892.
Source Type: Described photograph.
The photograph shows a large spinning room filled with rows of machines. Men and women stand near the frames while a supervisor watches from a raised platform. Open windows let in light, but the room appears crowded. A caption printed below the photograph reads: “Indian Capital and Modern Industry: Bombay Cotton Mills.”
Sourcing cues: Visual source likely produced for commercial or civic promotion; shows industrial labor and Indian-owned or locally financed factory production.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Editorial in an Egyptian reform newspaper, Cairo, 1895.
Source Type: Newspaper editorial.
“Cotton has brought money, railways, and foreign attention, but it has also tied the countryside to debts and prices set beyond Egypt. A nation cannot build strength from one crop alone. Schools, workshops, and local manufactures must grow, or the wealth of cotton will pass through our hands to bankers and merchants elsewhere.”
Sourcing cues: Reformist newspaper perspective; critiques dependency on export agriculture and calls for diversified development.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which mechanized textile production and global cotton demand transformed labor systems and regional economies from c. 1750 to 1900. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that cotton industrialization reorganized artisan labor, factory work, export agriculture, imperial policy, and regional dependency, while older rural labor and subsistence concerns persisted.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to cotton and industrialization, such as the Industrial Revolution, mechanized spinning and weaving, European imperialism, the growth of global markets, plantation agriculture, the American Civil War, steam transportation, colonial rule in South Asia, or state-sponsored industrialization.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about handloom decline, factory labor, raw cotton supply, export agriculture, rural taxation, imperial cotton policy, market disruption, Bombay mills, or economic dependency.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the spinning jenny, power loom, cotton gin, Manchester textile mills, slavery in the American South, the American Civil War cotton famine, British colonial economic policy in India, Egyptian cotton under Muhammad Ali or his successors, or Japanese textile industrialization.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why weavers emphasized debt and skill loss, why mill owners prioritized cheap cotton and markets, why village headmen worried about food supply, why commercial tables highlight market shifts, why Russian officials framed cotton as imperial strategy, or why reform newspapers criticized dependency.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of cotton and industrialization. This may include explaining that industrialization created both factory employment and artisan displacement, that cotton demand linked distant regions unevenly, that states tried to secure supply through imperial policy, or that export growth could bring railways and revenue while increasing dependency and food insecurity.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that mechanized textile production and global cotton demand transformed labor and regional economies by weakening some artisan producers, expanding factory work, increasing demand for raw cotton, encouraging export monoculture, and prompting states to integrate cotton-growing regions into imperial economies. Documents 1 and 6 are useful for comparing handloom decline and factory production. Documents 2, 4, and 5 show industrial demand and global market shifts. Documents 3 and 7 show rural and nationalist concerns about cotton dependency. Outside evidence could include the spinning jenny, power loom, cotton gin, Manchester, slavery in the American South, the American Civil War cotton famine, or British colonial policy in India. Strong sourcing should compare artisans, industrialists, village officials, commercial newspapers, imperial officials, visual promoters, and reform journalists.
